title: Huge Performance Difference Importing MySQL Dump: Docker vs Podman on macOS
body:
## Description

I am experiencing a massive difference in execution time. Podman is  **12x slower** than Docker when importing a MySQL, even though:

-  Same host machine (macOS)
-  The same Docker image is used `mysql:8.0`
-  The same database schema and data are imported
-  Both Docker and Podman have the same CPU and memory resources allocated (10 cores and 8GB memory)


## Steps to Reproduce

The sql schema is a single table with three integer or float fields. Data is 40M rows.

```
podman pull mysql:8.0
podman run -d --name test-database-1 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123 -p 3306:3306 mysql:8.0
podman exec -i test-database-1 mysql -u root -p123 main < data.sql
```

The exec command runs in **59m 57s**

```
docker pull mysql:8.0
docker run -d --name test-database-1 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123 -p 3306:3306 mysql:8.0
docker exec -i test-database-1 mysql -u root -p123 main < data.sql
```
The exec command runs in **4m 58s**

Also tried copying the file into the container and running from there but same outcome.


## podman info

comments.body:
The issue is that `stdin` is streamed through SSH to the VM, which creates significant overhead.

However, there is a workaround where you can SSH directly into the Podman machine to skip the network transfers. The user's home directory is mounted to the Podman machine, so the command should look like this:
```
podman machine ssh -- podman exec -i test-database-1 mysql -u root -p123 main < $(realpath data.sql)
```
For this specific use case, I believe this is the only workaround. I'm not sure if it's possible to solve this in a better way. 

WDYT? @Luap99 @mheon

Is the bottleneck really ssh here? What about the remote client copy performance into the socket and the server side from the socket? Does `podman exec --detach-keys ""` improve things? That should disable the (maybe expensive) byte comparison of each byte in the stream? I assume there are quite a lot of buffer copies happing on the path from the client into the container.

> podman machine ssh -- podman exec -i test-database-1 mysql -u root -p123 main < $(realpath data.sql)

Doesn't this still stream the file via ssh? The `< file` syntax is evaluated by the shell on the host which runs podman machine ssh so it is still piped over the network. I assume you would want to pipe the file from the VM on the mounted virtiofs volume to really avoid ssh.

@apphancer Also how large is the sql file exactly in bytes? I guess we could setup a test file and run  simply run `cat >/dev/null` as exec process to avoid any db overhead messing with the benchmark numbers and just measure the file transfer speed.

The file is 1.6GB. 

I've tried also copying the file into the container. Running `mysql -u root -p123 main < data.sql` directly inside the container seems to have similar performance issues. Finishing the test to run and I can update this with the result.

> 
> > podman machine ssh -- podman exec -i test-database-1 mysql -u root -p123 main < $(realpath data.sql)
> 
> Doesn't this still stream the file via ssh? The `< file` syntax is evaluated by the shell on the host which runs podman machine ssh so it is still piped over the network. I assume you would want to pipe the file from the VM on the mounted virtiofs volume to really avoid ssh.

Good catch. I forgot to enclose that command in quotes. Fixed version:
```
podman machine ssh -- "podman exec -i test-database-1 mysql -u root -p123 main < $(realpath data.sql)"
```

> 
> I've tried also copying the file into the container. Running `mysql -u root -p123 main < data.sql` directly inside the container seems to have similar performance issues. Finishing the test to run and I can update this with the result.

I suspect that some podman configuration is limiting the container's performance.
Running mysql `-u root -p123 main < data.sql` directly inside the container runs in 35 minutes. That's a big improvement, but still 7x slower compare to the same config in Docker.

> I suspect that some podman configuration is limiting the container's performance.

YEs, if the file is already in the container then there is really nothing left of podman itself in terms of processing overhead.
It could only be related to the podman container config, I wonder if there is are some seccomp syscalls we block or maybe the pid limit is to low so the db cannot create enough threads? Do you see any difference when creating the container with `--privileged` and `--pids-limit -1`?

And just to be sure you are using a native arm64 image not the know to be slow x86_64 emulation by any chance?
@cfergeau any ideas here ?

Found the culprit.
I was using the default provider type which is LibKrun. Switched to Apple HyperVisor and now the command runs in 3m 32s
The performance comparison has now reversed, with Podman much faster than Docker.
I was creating the machine from Podman Desktop as well as Podman cli, in both cases I was getting the poor performance. 
Not sure how to choose the provider type using cli, but in Podman Desktop there is a dropdown which defaults to LibKrun.

> Not sure how to choose the provider type using cli, but in Podman Desktop there is a dropdown which defaults to LibKrun.

`CONTAINERS_MACHINE_PROVIDER=applehv podman machine init` or you can define it like this in `containers.conf`:
```
[MACHINE]
provider = "applehv"
```

Interestingly I don't see this mentioned in our docs anywhere, also interesting is I thought applehv was the default hypervisor on Macs. 

@baude @Luap99 Is this slow performance expected when using LibKrun or should we report this to them?
@ninja-quokka applehv is the default for podman, but podman desktop recently changed *their* defaults to libkrun.
if someone from the team can reproduce this and we can agree on a reproducer, I think the libkrun team would be very interested.  same with PD @benoitf @jakecorrenti
I tried to reproduce that but couldn't. I think I'm missing a configuration setting, as I'm getting the following error: `ERROR 1153 (08S01): Got a packet bigger than 'max_allowed_packet' bytes`.

I worked with Copilot to create a simple script that generates an SQL file. However, I'm not sure if it's better to put 40 million entries into a single INSERT statement or split them into multiple ones. I suspect this choice is causing my error. 

So, could you provide more information about the SQL file? @apphancer 

Script:
```
#!/bin/bash
N=${1:-1000000}
cat>data.sql<<EOF
CREATE DATABASE main;USE main;
CREATE TABLE test_table(id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,value1 INT,value2 FLOAT,value3 DECIMAL(10,2),INDEX(value1));
INSERT INTO test_table(value1,value2,value3)VALUES
EOF
for((i=1;i<=N;i++));do printf "(%d,%d.%02d,%d.%02d)%s" $((RANDOM%1000)) $((RANDOM%1000)) $((RANDOM%100)) $((RANDOM%10000)) $((RANDOM%100)) $([[ $i -eq $N ]] && echo ";" || echo ",");((i%100000==0))&&echo "Generated $i rows...";done>>data.sql
echo "Created data.sql with $N rows"
```
Usage:
```
 ./gen.sh <number of rows>
```

@Honny1 
This is the table structure

```sql
CREATE TABLE `uprn` (
  `id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `latitude` decimal(10,7) NOT NULL,
  `longitude` decimal(10,7) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`,`latitude`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_unicode_ci;
```

The SQL file contains 40 million rows, batched into groups of 500 rows per INSERT statement.

Example:
```sql
INSERT INTO `uprn` (`id`, `latitude`, `longitude`) VALUES
(1, 51.4526038, -2.6020703),
(2, 51.5266333, -2.6793612),
(3, 51.5266333, -2.6793612),
...
(500, 51.5266333, -2.6793612);
```
could we not pipe any information that is large across and observe the same thing? or do we suspect it's a multiprocess thing wheer mysql is exposing that
@apphancer if you can provide the output of the machine being created with `podman machine inspect [podman-machine-name]`

I think the MySQL client will create 80,001 requests to the MySQL server for 40 million rows, as each SQL command is a single request. If each request uses one thread on the server side, libkrun might have problems with processes that have a lot of threads.
No x86_64 binaries involved in what you are testing? The x86_64 emulation is one big difference between libkrun and applehv (when it uses rosetta)

I think each execution of an SQL command will cause one request to the server with a new connection. But I am not exactly sure; I vaguely remember this from school, where I played around with databases. 

@apphancer But can you please verify it if you have your setup ready?
can you post `podman --log-level debug` for `podman run -d --name test-database-1 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123 -p 3306:3306 mysql:8.0`  ?

@Honny1 I can confirm that the inserts are run sequentially. When the import is running, the threads running are 2 (that is one more than the baseline state).

Here is the output of `podman stats <container>`

```
ID            NAME        CPU %       MEM USAGE / LIMIT  MEM %       NET IO             BLOCK IO         PIDS        CPU TIME      AVG CPU %
8c2107561687  db2         20.35%      613.2MB / 3.788GB  16.19%      6.877MB / 7.762MB  80.5MB / 11.1GB  42          4m53.948409s  13.49%
```

@baude 
I've run this with mysql:8.4

```
❯ podman --log-level debug run -d --name db4 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123 -p 3310:3306 mysql:8.4

INFO[0000] podman filtering at log level debug
DEBU[0000] Called run.PersistentPreRunE(podman --log-level debug run -d --name db4 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123 -p 3310:3306 mysql:8.4)
DEBU[0000] SSH Ident Key "/Users/m/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/machine" SHA256:za637uu9+ZMvJaIWS3uOEsd2o6nCFPIgvL4CiW+Q52E ssh-ed25519
DEBU[0000] DoRequest Method: GET URI: http://d/v5.6.1/libpod/_ping
DEBU[0000] Adding port mapping from 3310 to 3306 length 1 protocol ""
DEBU[0000] Loading registries configuration "/etc/containers/registries.conf"
DEBU[0000] DoRequest Method: POST URI: http://d/v5.6.1/libpod/images/pull
DEBU[0000] User or group ID mappings not available: open /proc/self/uid_map: no such file or directory
DEBU[0000] User or group ID mappings not available: open /proc/self/uid_map: no such file or directory
DEBU[0000] DoRequest Method: GET URI: http://d/v5.6.1/libpod/images/mysql:8.4/json
DEBU[0000] DoRequest Method: POST URI: http://d/v5.6.1/libpod/containers/create
DEBU[0000] DoRequest Method: POST URI: http://d/v5.6.1/libpod/containers/473d5c8bf6b1c08ab2c30d78cbd3fb44e79d0fb92668e7b8e177490c2bd3c9a1/start
473d5c8bf6b1c08ab2c30d78cbd3fb44e79d0fb92668e7b8e177490c2bd3c9a1
DEBU[0000] Called run.PersistentPostRunE(podman --log-level debug run -d --name db4 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123 -p 3310:3306 mysql:8.4)
DEBU[0000] Shutting down engines
```
@apphancer just speaking for @Honny1 here, but we think we have reproduced the problem.  thanks for sticking with us!

I was able to reproduce this issue using libkrun (v1.0.0), with all commands executed inside a VM to minimize potential client-server problems.

It appears that libkrun struggles with I/O utilization. I used the default VM configurations created by Podman for testing. During a database import, `podman stats` showed CPU usage for the `libkrun` VM at only 20%, compared to 70% for the `AppleHV` VM. I also observed very low disk I/O utilization with libkrun, peaking at 35 MB/s, while `AppleHV `reached 185 MB/s.

To isolate the problem, I ran the tests with the SQL file copied directly into the VM user's home directory, which suggests the issue is indeed related to I/O performance. I then ran `fio` benchmarks, and the results are below.

`AppleHV` is faster than `libkrun` when accessing the VM's disk. I wasn't able to reproduce the exact MySQL behavior with the `fio` benchmark, but I think these results still show a significant performance gap in IO operations. 

WDYT? @baude

I'll measure it.

@Luap99 Same benchmark, but with a directory mounted using `virtiofs`.

## Libkrun:
```
core@localhost:~$ sudo fio --name=mysql_simulation --ioengine=libaio --rw=randwrite --bs=16k --direct=1 --size=1G --numjobs=4 --runtime=60 --group_reporting --filename=/Users/jrodak/io_test

...
fio-3.40
Starting 4 processes
mysql_simulation: Laying out IO file (1 file / 1024MiB)
```

> `AppleHV` is faster than `libkrun` when accessing the VM's disk.

The virtualization framework (which vfkit/applehv use) allows to specify the caching behaviour for virtio-blk IOs:
https://developer.apple.com/documentation/virtualization/vzdiskimagecachingmode?language=objc
https://developer.apple.com/documentation/virtualization/vzdiskimagesynchronizationmode?language=objc

These parameters are currently hardcoded to Cached/FSync: https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/blob/88ab5cdcf4447875ac0a555072bb7441f3527a5f/pkg/vf/virtio.go#L493-L494

I don’t know if this could explain the difference with krunkit.

> > `AppleHV` is faster than `libkrun` when accessing the VM's disk.
> 
> The virtualization framework (which vfkit/applehv use) allows to specify the caching behaviour for virtio-blk IOs: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/virtualization/vzdiskimagecachingmode?language=objc https://developer.apple.com/documentation/virtualization/vzdiskimagesynchronizationmode?language=objc
> 
> These parameters are currently hardcoded to Cached/FSync: https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/blob/88ab5cdcf4447875ac0a555072bb7441f3527a5f/pkg/vf/virtio.go#L493-L494
> 
> I don’t know if this could explain the difference with krunkit.

That's what I'm investigating right now, because it's likely to be the main reason (there doesn't seem to be a huge difference in reads, only in writes, and it's very likely that InnoDB opens the files with O_DIRECT, inhibiting the guest's cache). In libkrun we're pretty conservative regarding disk writes, mostly because on flushing data on macOS wasn't very reliable in the past (I remember VF had to disable cache at some point precisely for this reason). It's also "The Right Way" (TM), as otherwise we wouldn't be honoring O_DIRECT, but if users want it, we can offer the option to enable write cache on virtio-blk devices.

> mostly because on flushing data on macOS wasn't very reliable in the past (I remember VF had to disable cache at some point precisely for this reason)

This was https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/commit/732de99a184e83c11e245f4f0d4e03ccedddb4f5 , the default must be "auto", which caused corruptions in some cases. Haven’t tried to change it back to see if it improved.

As suspected, VF is aggressively caching disk writes (notice how they're grouped in bursts), effectively breaking InnoDB's O_DIRECT: As a comparison, this is how it looks with libkrun: Let's try enabling write cache in libkrun's virtio-blk to confirm the performance gap is closed.

I'm running the following test:

- Create the container, the DB, and connect to it:

```
podman pull mysql:8.0
podman run -d --name test-database-1 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123 -p 3306:3306 mysql:8.0
podman exec -i test-database-1 mysqladmin -u root -p123 create main
podman exec -ti test-database-1 mysql -u root -p123 main
```

- Create a table and load random data on it:
```
mysql> CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS customers (  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,  customer_name TEXT,  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ) ENGINE=INNODB;

mysql> INSERT INTO customers (id, customer_name) SELECT n, CONCAT('Customer', n)  FROM ( select a.N + b.N * 10 + c.N *
100 + d.N * 1000 + e.N * 10000 + f.N * 100000 + g.N * 1000000 + 1 N from (select 0 as N union all select 1 union all select 2 union all select 3 union all select 4 union all select 5 union all select 6 union all select 7 union all select
8 union all select 9) a  , (select 0 as N union all select 1 union all select 2 union all select 3 union all select 4 union all select 5 union all select 6 union all select 7 union all select 8 union all select 9) b  , (select 0 as N union all select 1 union all select 2 union all select 3 union all select 4 union all select 5 union all select 6 union all select 7 union all select 8 union all select 9) c  , (select 0 as N union all select 1 union all select 2 union all select 3 union all select 4 union all select 5 union all select 6 union all select 7 union all select 8 union all select
9) d  , (select 0 as N union all select 1 union all select 2 union all select 3 union all select 4 union all select 5 union all select 6 union all select 7 union all select 8 union all select 9) e  , (select 0 as N union all select 1 union all select 2 union all select 3 union all select 4 union all select 5 union all select 6 union all select 7 union all select 8 union all select 9) f  , (select 0 as N union all select 1 union all select 2 union all select 3 union all select 4 union all select 5 union all select 6 union all select 7 union all select 8 union all select 9) g ) t;
```

And these are the numbers:

| VMM | Time to complete INSERT |
| ----- | ------------------------ |
| VF | 36.35 sec |
| libkrun | 6 min 4.78 sec |
| libkrun with relaxed fsync() | 38.29 sec |

On macOS, Rust's `std::fs::File::flush()` uses `F_FULLFSYNC`, which syncs the buffers to the drive AND asks the drive to wait for flush all buffered data to the permanent storage. This is what we're using in libkrun through [imago](https://gitlab.com/hreitz/imago). On the other hand, Apple's Virtualization.framework with `VZDiskImageSynchronizationModeFsync` only issues a `fsync()`, which doesn't ask the drive to flush its buffered data.

Strictly speaking, for running DBs, `F_FULLFSYNC` should be used, as otherwise the risk of data loss is quite significant. But I guess most people aren't running production DBs on macOS, using them for testing and developing purposes instead, so perhaps it'd be reasonable defaulting to a softer `fsync()`. WDYT @baude ?

I’ve created https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/pull/379 to get some vfkit builds with the default caching behaviour to see if they make a difference in data reliability/performance.

Edit: Binary is available from https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/actions/runs/18312806027/artifacts/4203332334 if someone  wants to test it
https://github.com/apple/containerization/pull/446 Apple container defaults to `cache=auto`, `sync=fsync` for these settings.
